status: first-pass evidence bundle exported from tracker rows

observations.observation_value: Chronic rat hemisection scar/injury region had lower Young's modulus than naive tissue; uninjured region averaged about 2.6-fold higher than around injury.
observations.normalized_value: scar softening vs naive tissue
observations.statistics_reported: p < 0.0001; Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon; N = 3 independent tissues for Fig 1 summary
observations.notes: Fresh 500 um sections, 80 um bead, right lateral hemisection, 15-18 WPI; source quote: "2.6-fold higher".

observations.observation_value: Chronic hemisection scar contained cavitation/syrinx areas interspersed with dense, disorganized nuclei packing.
observations.normalized_value: cavitary scar architecture with dense nuclei regions
observations.statistics_reported: DNA content decreased; nuclei spacing quantified in source
observations.notes: Supports structural explanation for altered mechanics; source quote: "areas of cavitation".

observations.observation_value: Scar tissue showed loss/dysregulation of dominant CNS cell markers with vimentin upregulation.
observations.normalized_value: reduced CNS cell marker profile; increased vimentin
observations.statistics_reported: IHC quantified by normalized intensity
observations.notes: Markers included beta-III tubulin, GFAP, myelin/antigalactocerebroside, tenascin R, vimentin, and Hoechst.

observations.observation_value: Total sGAGs decreased in scar-containing tissue, while targeted CSPG analysis showed roughly two-fold increase versus naive tissue.
observations.normalized_value: total sGAG down; CSPG subset up
observations.statistics_reported: Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn's multiple comparison
observations.notes: Shows ECM components diverge by GAG subclass; source quote: "nearly a two-fold increase".

observations.observation_value: Hyaluronic acid was upregulated in chronic scar tissue.
observations.normalized_value: HA upregulated in chronic scar
observations.statistics_reported: Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn's multiple comparison
observations.notes: Authors interpret GAG/HA dysregulation as likely relevant to elastic behavior.

observations.observation_value: Frozen/thawed and fresh uninjured mouse spinal cord preparations did not differ significantly in measured elastic modulus.
observations.normalized_value: sample preparation comparison showed no significant difference
observations.statistics_reported: p = 0.3823; Mann-Whitney test
observations.notes: Important method-control observation because Cooper used 10 um cryosections on slides.

observations.observation_value: Chronic severe mouse contusion lesion regions were stiffer than uninjured spinal cord.
observations.normalized_value: chronic contusion stiffening vs uninjured
observations.statistics_reported: significance reported in Fig 2 / source tables
observations.notes: Severe T11 contusion, 12 WPI, female C57BL/6 mice.

observations.observation_value: Mouse and human astrocytes cultured on 2000 Pa substrate showed stiffness-associated morphology changes relative to 200 Pa substrate.
observations.normalized_value: astrocytes respond to scar-range stiffness
observations.statistics_reported: human circularity p < 0.01; complexity p < 0.0001
observations.notes: Supports plausibility that tissue mechanics can influence glial phenotype; source quote: "2000 Pa substrate".

observations.observation_value: Overall lesion-site stiffness after IMP treatment was 517 Pa versus 1217 Pa in saline-treated injured mice and 405 Pa in uninjured spinal cord.
observations.normalized_value: IMP reduced chronic lesion stiffness toward uninjured values
observations.statistics_reported: p = 0.0180; Kruskal-Wallis test
observations.notes: Directly links reduced fibrotic scarring intervention to reduced stiffness.

observations.observation_value: In IMP-treated mice, lesion rim was stiffer than lesion core: 601 Pa versus 434 Pa.
observations.normalized_value: IMP-treated rim > core stiffness
observations.statistics_reported: p = 0.0432; Mann-Whitney test
observations.notes: Indicates subregion mechanics remain spatially heterogeneous after treatment.

observations.observation_value: Saline-treated chronic contusion scars showed highly aligned GFAP-positive fibers; IMP-treated tissue had reduced alignment approaching uninjured controls.
observations.normalized_value: IMP reduced pathological glial fiber alignment
observations.statistics_reported: p < 0.01; one-way ANOVA with Tukey post hoc; n = 5 mice/group
observations.notes: CurveAlign measured glial/fibrotic interface alignment; source quote: "p < 0.01".

observations.observation_value: In uninjured mouse spinal cord, Col1alpha1-positive cells localized mainly along larger blood vessels, expressed CD13 and PDGFRbeta, and lacked NG2, nestin, alphaSMA, immune, endothelial, astrocyte, oligodendrocyte, and fibrocyte markers.
observations.normalized_value: Col1alpha1-positive perivascular fibroblast-like baseline population
observations.statistics_reported: 60.9 +/- 5.1% around smooth muscle cells; 78.7% CD13-positive; 95.7% PDGFRbeta-positive
observations.notes: Supports distinction from NG2/nestin pericyte marker profile; source quote: "preferentially around larger-diameter blood vessels".

observations.observation_value: After T8 contusion, Col1alpha1-positive cells accumulated from 4-5 DPI, peaked at 7 DPI, and by 14 DPI formed a fibrous scar apposed to the GFAP-positive astrocytic border; 5-HT axons did not penetrate the Col1alpha1-positive region.
observations.normalized_value: delayed Col1alpha1-positive fibrotic scar formation with axon-excluding border
observations.statistics_reported: one-way ANOVA, p < 0.0001 for cell-density time course
observations.notes: Female Col1alpha1-GFP mice, 75 kdyn T8 contusion, dura largely intact.

observations.observation_value: Col1alpha1-positive scar cells remained broadly PDGFRbeta-positive after contusion, CD13 expression decreased by 14 DPI, alphaSMA was transient and low by 14 DPI, and nonfibroblast lineage markers remained absent.
observations.normalized_value: PDGFRbeta-positive Col1alpha1 scar cells with dynamic CD13/alphaSMA phenotype
observations.statistics_reported: 100% PDGFRbeta-positive; 26.9% CD13-positive at 14 DPI; about 3.09% alphaSMA-positive at 14 DPI
observations.notes: Important because PDGFRbeta labels multiple perivascular populations and cannot alone define origin.

observations.observation_value: Dorsal hemisection and contusion had similar timing of Col1alpha1-positive cell accumulation, but hemisection produced a Col1alpha1-positive scar continuous with dura and a laminin distribution throughout the injury site rather than mainly peripheral rim.
observations.normalized_value: dural disruption shifts fibrotic scar geometry toward meningeal continuity
observations.statistics_reported: n = 3 per dorsal hemisection time point
observations.notes: Boundary condition for contusion versus penetrating injury origin.

observations.observation_value: NG2-lineage cells localized mostly outside the Col1alpha1-positive injury epicenter after contusion, and red NG2-lineage cells rarely overlapped with green Col1alpha1-positive scar cells.
observations.normalized_value: NG2-positive pericytes/OPCs not major Col1alpha1-positive scar source
observations.statistics_reported: n = 3 at 14 DPI; rare overlap noted
observations.notes: Authors caution NG2-CreER labels only a fraction of NG2-positive pericytes.

observations.observation_value: Tamoxifen-induced Glast-Rasless recombination reduced PDGFRbeta-positive stromal scar occupancy after dorsal hemisection; complete blockade in 3 of 12 tamoxifen-treated mice caused failure to close the injury site, whereas 9 of 12 showed partial attenuation with tissue integrity.
observations.normalized_value: partial type A pericyte-derived scar reduction; complete block produces tissue defect
observations.statistics_reported: 3/12 Tam-def; 9/12 Tam intermediate; PDGFRbeta scar occupancy quantified
observations.notes: Separates beneficial attenuation from harmful ablation.

observations.observation_value: At 2 WPI, vehicle versus tamoxifen lesion RNA-seq identified 1,099 differentially expressed genes enriched for ECM organization, collagen fibril organization, adhesion, angiogenesis, and wound healing; fibrosis-associated genes were reduced in Tam animals and clustered with uninjured profiles.
observations.normalized_value: broad ECM/fibrosis transcriptional program depends on type A pericyte progeny
observations.statistics_reported: FDR-adjusted p < 0.05; fold change > +/-1.5
observations.notes: Supports mechanism beyond PDGFRbeta area alone.

observations.observation_value: Moderate reduction of pericyte-derived scarring reduced CST dieback/retraction features and allowed CST axons to enter or extend beyond lesion tissue at chronic time points, whereas vehicle and tissue-defect animals showed little or no regeneration.
observations.normalized_value: moderate scar attenuation permits CST regeneration
observations.statistics_reported: 10.5 +/- 2.1% traced CST axons reached lesion site in Tam animals at 18 WPI
observations.notes: Tam-def tissue defects did not improve CST regeneration, reinforcing the partial-attenuation boundary.

observations.observation_value: In scar-attenuated mice with ChR2-labeled CST axons, photostimulation evoked postsynaptic activity caudal to the lesion, supporting functional integration of regenerated CST projections.
observations.normalized_value: regenerated CST axons functionally integrate below lesion
observations.statistics_reported: electrophysiology and optogenetic stimulation reported
observations.notes: Source conclusion links regenerated axons to local spinal circuitry below lesion.

observations.observation_value: Animals with attenuated pericyte-derived scarring showed improved sensorimotor recovery, and regenerated axon number correlated with behavioral improvement.
observations.normalized_value: scar attenuation-associated axon growth tracks recovery
observations.statistics_reported: correlation analyses reported; horizontal ladder, CatWalk, and ChR2-assisted behavior used
observations.notes: Behavioral analysis excluded animals with spared CST fibers and tissue defects.

observations.observation_value: PDGFRbeta-lineage cells were recruited into lesion cores after both transection and crush SCI, with higher density after transection; many proliferated at 5 DPI and about half were BrdU-positive by 14 DPI.
observations.normalized_value: PDGFRbeta-positive stromal recruitment is injury-model dependent
observations.statistics_reported: 49.18 +/- 2.86% Ki67-positive after transection and 43.26 +/- 2.24% after crush at 5 DPI
observations.notes: Transection is penetrating; crush is non-penetrating with intact dura.

observations.observation_value: PDGFRbeta-positive lesion cells included fibroblasts, dividing fibroblasts, and pericytes/vSMCs, but Col1a2-lineage fibroblasts overlapped strongly with Col1-positive scar regions whereas Myh11- and NG2-lineage pericyte/vSMC cells contributed little to the lesion core.
observations.normalized_value: fibroblasts, not Myh11/NG2 pericytes/vSMCs, primary scar source in this lineage system
observations.statistics_reported: 44,386 PDGFRbeta-lineage scRNA-seq cells; lineage-density comparisons reported
observations.notes: Directly boundary-checks pericyte/vSMC interpretation.

observations.observation_value: Meningeal Crabp2/Emb fibroblasts localized mainly to central fibrotic scar regions, while perivascular/pial Lama1/Lama2 fibroblasts localized mainly to peripheral/perivascular scar regions after transection and crush SCI.
observations.normalized_value: CE-F central; LA-F peripheral/perivascular
observations.statistics_reported: Emb/Lama1 and Crabp2/Lama2 areas quantified at 14 DPI
observations.notes: Adds subcompartment structure within the fibrotic scar.

observations.observation_value: Rhesus macaque transection lesions showed fibroblast subtype organization comparable to mouse SCI, including CE-F-like and LA-F-like populations in lesion core/edge samples.
observations.normalized_value: mouse fibroblast scar subtypes have monkey analogs
observations.statistics_reported: female rhesus macaques sampled at 0, 7, 14, 30 DPI, and 6 MPI
observations.notes: Supports translational relevance but in complete transection monkey model.

observations.observation_value: CE-F showed stronger cholesterol synthesis and type I collagen/fibronectin programs, while LA-F showed laminin/type IV collagen, lipid transport/storage, and angiogenesis-associated function including stronger endothelial tube formation support.
observations.normalized_value: CE-F and LA-F have distinct ECM/metabolic/vascular functions
observations.statistics_reported: GO enrichment, Bodipy/myelin uptake, RT-qPCR, tube formation, and ligand-receptor analyses reported
observations.notes: Functionalizes scar compartments instead of treating fibrosis as one cell state.

observations.observation_value: After T9 contusion, GFAP-positive astrocytic scar area and astrocyte process overlap increased over time and persisted to 12 WPI; chronic astrocytes differed transcriptionally from subacute scar-forming astrocytes.
observations.normalized_value: chronic glial scar persists but changes phenotype over time
observations.statistics_reported: p < 0.05 threshold; group comparisons reported
observations.notes: Chronic astrocytes had higher Sox9 but lower Cdh2, Csgalanact1, and Pcan than 2 WPI scar-forming astrocytes.

observations.observation_value: IL-6 reactive astrocytes converted to collagen-I-grown scar-forming astrocytes on type I collagen; after reseeding onto Poly-D-lysine, Col-I-SAs retained flattened morphology, N-cadherin expression, and SA-marker gene expression.
observations.normalized_value: once induced, Col-I-SA phenotype resists collagen-signal withdrawal
observations.statistics_reported: n = 6 wells per group; ANOVA/Tukey-Kramer and Wilcoxon tests
observations.notes: Indicates scar-forming astrocyte state is not simply reversed by removing the initiating collagen surface.

observations.observation_value: Anti-beta1-integrin or N-cadherin-neutralizing antibody treatment did not revert Col-I-SA morphology or SA-marker gene expression to reactive or naive astrocyte states in vitro.
observations.normalized_value: established Col-I-SAs resist beta1-integrin/N-cadherin blockade in vitro
observations.statistics_reported: n = 6 wells per group; Wilcoxon rank-sum tests
observations.notes: Distinguishes prevention of transformation from reversal of established scar astrocytes.

observations.observation_value: EGFP-positive Col-I-SAs transplanted into naive spinal cord formed N-cadherin-rich astroglial scar-like clusters and induced nearby host GFAP-positive astrocytes to express SA-associated genes.
observations.normalized_value: scar-forming astrocytes self-maintain and recruit adjacent astrocytes
observations.statistics_reported: n = 6 mice per group; Wilcoxon rank-sum tests
observations.notes: Host astrocytes increased Cdh2, Sox9, Acan, and Pcan after transplantation.

observations.observation_value: Anti-beta1-integrin antibody blocked recruitment of host scar-forming astrocytes after SA transplantation and, when injected from 14-28 DPI after SCI, reduced GFAP/N-cadherin-positive chronic astrocytes and increased GAP43-positive area.
observations.normalized_value: beta1-integrin blockade reduces chronic SA recruitment and increases GAP43 signal
observations.statistics_reported: n = 6 mice per group; Wilcoxon rank-sum tests; p < 0.05
observations.notes: Supports intervention on recruitment rather than direct in vitro reversion.

observations.observation_value: Cx3cr1-CreER INTACT profiling identified temporal injury-activated microglia/macrophage signatures at 3, 7, and 14 DPI after T8 dorsal column transection.
observations.normalized_value: temporal IAM program after SCI
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Included because astrocytic border formation is coupled to immune corralling and wound compaction.

observations.observation_value: Myeloid Plexin-B2 deletion disrupted lesion-core corralling, astrocytic border containment, matrix compaction, inflammatory restriction, axon regeneration, and motor-sensory recovery after dorsal column transection.
observations.normalized_value: myeloid Plexin-B2 promotes wound compaction and astrocytic containment
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Bridges M1B-T003 with immune-glial containment topic M1B-T008.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that astrocytic Ryk is induced after SCI and that astrocyte-focused Ryk perturbation alters morphological transformation, scarring, wound architecture, and lesion-core containment.
observations.normalized_value: astrocytic Ryk coordinates border formation and wound healing
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Needs full-text parsing before high-confidence observation materialization.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that border-forming astrocytes after SCI or stroke arise mainly from local astrocytes, with a minority contribution from OPC-derived astrocytes.
observations.normalized_value: local astrocytes are major border-forming source; OPCs minor source
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Complements Tamaru's recruitment model with origin tracing.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports temporal transcriptomic and single-nucleus RNA-seq definition of border-forming wound repair astrocyte programs after SCI/stroke.
observations.normalized_value: border astrocytes are a wound-repair transcriptional state
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Supports classification of astrocytic scar as an active wound-repair compartment.

observations.observation_value: Gong generated a T10 lateral hemisection spatial transcriptomic atlas across 3, 7, 14, and 28 DPI using 32 sections and 15,449 spots, mapping clusters back to tissue morphology to define scar regions.
observations.normalized_value: spatially mapped scar maturation atlas
observations.statistics_reported: 32 sections; 15,449 spots; 3, 7, 14, 28 DPI
observations.notes: Provides architecture scaffold for Module 1B compartment definitions.

observations.observation_value: Reclustered scar spots resolved six major scar-resident cell groups: microglia, macrophages, astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, fibroblasts, and endothelial cells; fibroblasts occupied the scar center from 7 DPI, with microglia, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes arranged outward toward the boundary.
observations.normalized_value: multicellular scar with fibroblast core and glial/immune boundary layers
observations.statistics_reported: Scar-cell fractions quantified across maturation stages
observations.notes: Source also notes very few neurons in the scar area.

observations.observation_value: Laminin, Fn1, collagen IV staining and ST maps showed changing scar marker distributions; fibroblasts remained central during shrinkage/maturation, while microglial and astrocytic scar regions expanded along white matter at 28 DPI.
observations.normalized_value: ECM marker maps define dynamic scar boundary and subcompartments
observations.statistics_reported: Cell-type spot counts and fractions quantified
observations.notes: Confirms fibrotic scar surrounded by microglial scar wrapped by astrocytic scar in this model.

observations.observation_value: Gong proposed four scar-formation phases after lateral hemisection: macrophage infiltration, proliferation/differentiation of scar-resident cells, scar emergence, and stationary scar.
observations.normalized_value: four-phase scar maturation model
observations.statistics_reported: WGCNA modules and cell-fraction trajectories support synthesis
observations.notes: Interpretive synthesis from spatial transcriptomics, not a direct single assay.

observations.observation_value: FnEDA and FnEDB were low/detectable in uninjured spinal cord, rose sharply after T11 contusion, and remained elevated even 13 weeks after injury.
observations.normalized_value: persistent cellular fibronectin isoform expression after SCI
observations.statistics_reported: FnEDA mRNA about 70-fold and FnEDB about 40-fold at 7 DPI; n = 8-10 for qPCR
observations.notes: FnEDA protein increased around 10-fold by 7 DPI and declined but remained elevated.

observations.observation_value: FnEDA-null mice had smaller GFAP-bounded lesion cores and lower fibronectin intensity inside the GFAP-negative lesion core at 90 DPI, while GFAP glial scarring was not reduced.
observations.normalized_value: FnEDA deletion reduces chronic fibrotic core without reducing glial rim
observations.statistics_reported: lesion area p < 0.01; fibronectin intensity p < 0.01
observations.notes: Supports separability of fibrotic core and astrocytic border compartments.

observations.observation_value: At 21 and 90 DPI, FnEDA-null mice had reduced insoluble matrix-assembled fibronectin while soluble fibronectin did not differ from wildtype.
observations.normalized_value: FnEDA stabilizes chronic assembled fibronectin matrix
observations.statistics_reported: n = 7-8 mice/group; p < 0.01
observations.notes: No difference in soluble/insoluble fibronectin at 7 DPI.

observations.observation_value: FnEDA-null mice had higher SMI-312 axonal density in lesion areas at 90 DPI and improved locomotor recovery by BMS and modified BBB compared with wildtype controls.
observations.normalized_value: reduced chronic fibrotic matrix associates with axon density and recovery
observations.statistics_reported: SMI-312 p < 0.05 or p < 0.01 by distance; behavior p < 0.05 to p < 0.001
observations.notes: Functional link supports chronic fibrotic compartment as recovery-relevant.

observations.observation_value: Abstract-level extraction reports chronic rat contusion repaired tissue organized into fibrotic, cellular, and axonal zones, and scar ablation plus lamina propria/acellular lamina propria transplantation expanded cellular/axonal zones, reduced cavity, and improved integration with spared tissue.
observations.normalized_value: chronic repaired tissue has zone architecture modifiable by ablation/transplantation
observations.statistics_reported: Abstract-level only
observations.notes: Full text needed before high-confidence materialization.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that single-cell RNA-seq resolved heterogeneous injured-spinal-cord scar-like cell states and inferred cell-cell interactions shaping the wound-healing/scar environment.
observations.normalized_value: scar is multicellular interaction ecosystem
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Supports T001/T008 but needs full-text extraction for exact cell-state names.

observations.observation_value: After incomplete SCI, lesion-remote astrocytes in spared rostral and caudal cord acquired molecularly distinct, neuroanatomically restricted reactivity states across 3, 7, 14, and 28 DPI.
observations.normalized_value: lesion-remote astrocytes are heterogeneous spatial states
observations.statistics_reported: n values reported per figure; multiple transcriptomic modalities
observations.notes: Distinct from border-forming astrocytes and nontraumatic astrocyte states.

observations.observation_value: WM3/4 lesion-remote astrocytes co-localized with Mg2/Mg5 white matter degeneration-associated microglia in ipsilesional Wallerian-degenerating white matter.
observations.normalized_value: WM3/4 LRA-WDM microglia niche
observations.statistics_reported: Spatial and snRNA-seq integration; WDM nodules quantified
observations.notes: Mg2/5 microglia phagocytose myelin debris and assemble into nodules.

observations.observation_value: Ccn1-positive white matter LRAs were restricted to Wallerian-degenerating white matter, neighbored debris-clearing WDM nodules, persisted to at least 90 DPI, and were required to restrain excessive/spatially aberrant microglial activation.
observations.normalized_value: Ccn1+ LRAs regulate WDM localization and magnitude
observations.statistics_reported: WDM nodules up to about fourfold increased in Ccn1-cKO regions
observations.notes: CCN1 loss amplified microglial response rather than eliminating it.

observations.observation_value: Astrocyte-specific Ccn1 deletion caused microglia to accumulate more internalized myelin and axon debris, attenuated debris clearance, and impaired neurological recovery after incomplete SCI.
observations.normalized_value: astrocyte CCN1 supports microglial debris processing and recovery
observations.statistics_reported: About 40% greater myelin debris and about 23% greater axon debris reported in Ccn1-cKO microglia
observations.notes: Demonstrates remote white matter repair affects outcome.

observations.observation_value: Recombinant CCN1 altered primary microglial lipid metabolism, including cholesterol efflux/lipid storage responses, supporting a mechanism for repair-associated lipid buffering.
observations.normalized_value: CCN1 modulates microglial lipid metabolism
observations.statistics_reported: RNA-seq, cholesterol efflux, LipidTOX, and lipidomics assays
observations.notes: Mechanistic bridge from astrocyte secreted factor to debris-clearing microglia.

observations.evidence_type: Myelin damage models / human tissue comparison
observations.observation_value: Local myelin damage induced Ccn1-positive astrocytes, and comparable white-matter damage-associated astrocyte patterns were observed across demyelinating models and human disorder tissue, including SCI-relevant lesion-remote white matter.
observations.normalized_value: Ccn1+ white matter astrocyte response is damage-linked and conserved
observations.statistics_reported: Multiple mouse injury/demyelination models and human tissues
observations.notes: Supports generality beyond the primary incomplete SCI model.

observations.observation_value: In five rat spines 2 months after compressive SCI, 9.4T inversion-recovery-supported proton-density MRI depicted spared and injured white matter, lesion volume, and cord atrophy; MRI severity and white-matter sparing aligned with locomotor recovery and histology.
observations.normalized_value: spared white matter is MRI-visible and recovery-linked
observations.statistics_reported: n = 5 excised rat spines
observations.notes: Post-mortem MRI; not in vivo longitudinal imaging.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that 7.0T MRI tracked pathological variation in white matter tracts after severe thoracic contusion, and spared white matter/pathological tract variation related to hindlimb motor recovery.
observations.normalized_value: MRI-visible white matter tract pathology relates to recovery
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Full text needed for exact MRI metric values.

observations.observation_value: Abstract-level extraction reports chronic changes in residual dorsal, lateral, and ventral funicular white matter cellular composition 6 WPI after contusion, with acute TTX treatment related to chronic axon density, function, and cellular composition.
observations.normalized_value: residual white matter has chronic cellular remodeling
observations.statistics_reported: Abstract-level only
observations.notes: Full text needed before high-confidence materialization.

observations.observation_value: In adult female rat moderate contusion SCI, delayed 2 mm glial scar resection at 1 WPI produced significantly worse BBB locomotor scores than nonresection contusion controls and did not produce BDA-positive axons at or caudal to the lesion epicenter.
observations.normalized_value: scar resection alone worsened contusion recovery and did not restore axon crossing
observations.statistics_reported: BBB p < 0.05 versus nonresection contusion; axon comparisons p values reported
observations.notes: Critical boundary condition against treating resection alone as inherently reparative.

observations.observation_value: In adult female rat dorsal hemisection SCI, delayed 2 mm glial scar resection at 1 WPI did not significantly change BBB locomotor scores versus nonresection hemisection controls, and no group showed BDA-positive axons at or caudal to the lesion epicenter.
observations.normalized_value: scar resection alone neutral after hemisection and insufficient for lesion crossing
observations.statistics_reported: BBB p = 0.61 for resection versus nonresection hemisection; BDA comparisons reported
observations.notes: Shows injury-model dependence and lack of axon-bridging benefit from resection alone.

observations.observation_value: Abstract-level extraction reports that, in 12-week chronic rat SCI, scar resection was evaluated for locomotor safety and NeuroRegen collagen scaffold plus hUC-MSC implantation into the resected cavity was assessed by locomotion, evoked potentials, and histological repair outcomes.
observations.normalized_value: chronic resection cavity used as scaffold/cell implantation site
observations.statistics_reported: Abstract-level only
observations.notes: Full text needed before exact effect sizes or histological endpoints are materialized.

observations.outcome_type: Scar boundary mapping and gap filling
observations.observation_value: In five complete chronic ASIA A SCI patients, SSEP/MEP monitoring was used to distinguish scar from normal neural tissue, scars ranging from 0.5-4.5 cm were resected, and BMMC-loaded NeuroRegen scaffold was implanted into the spinal cord gap.
observations.normalized_value: electrophysiology-guided human scar resection plus scaffold/cell filling
observations.statistics_reported: n = 5 patients; scar length table reported
observations.notes: MRI lesion length was sometimes smaller than intraoperative electrophysiological scar mapping.

observations.observation_value: Resected chronic human SCI tissue showed strong CSPG and vimentin staining and negative NF, nestin, Tuj-1, and S100 staining, supporting that the dissected tissue was scar-rich and neural-marker-poor.
observations.normalized_value: resected human scar is CSPG/vimentin-positive and neural-marker-poor
observations.statistics_reported: qualitative IHC
observations.notes: Supports surgical target identity for complete chronic SCI cases.

observations.observation_value: After human scar resection and BMMC-loaded NeuroRegen implantation, no obvious adverse effects related to resection or scaffold transplantation were observed immediately or at 12 months; two cases showed lower-limb SSEP recovery by 6 months that persisted to the end of observation.
observations.normalized_value: procedure feasible/safe in small cohort with partial SSEP recovery
observations.statistics_reported: n = 5; 2 cases with SSEP recovery
observations.notes: No neurological deterioration or spine instability was detected after scar resection.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that chronic complete SCI scar resection created a cavitary lesion repair setting where decellularized ECM hydrogel scaffold filling, with or without human iPSC-derived NS/PC transplantation, was assessed for histological repair effects.
observations.normalized_value: dECM scaffold and hNS/PC support after chronic scar resection
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Full text should be parsed for lesion filling, graft survival, and histological outcome details before high-confidence materialization.

observations.observation_value: Human SCI lesions changed rapidly from 3 days to 1 month: tissue bridge width increased from 0.68 mm to 1.41 mm, IMLA decreased 75%, and IMLL decreased 73%, then lesion metrics remained largely stable from 1 month to 5 years.
observations.normalized_value: early lesion demarcation then chronic geometry stability
observations.statistics_reported: TB p = 0.02; IMLA/IMLL p < 0.001; stable 1 month-5 years p = 0.28/0.30
observations.notes: Human acute-to-chronic imaging anchor for lesion geometry.

observations.observation_value: Over 5 years after human SCI, early C1-C3 and intracranial CST swelling with R2* increase and MTsat decrease progressed into C1-C3 and CST volume loss plus continued MTsat/motor-system microstructural changes.
observations.normalized_value: remote motor system shows chronic MRI-visible degeneration
observations.statistics_reported: significant FDR-corrected clusters reported
observations.notes: Supports chronic lesion effects beyond local epicenter.

observations.observation_value: Greater first-month tissue bridge increase correlated with motor recovery, while less C1-C3 atrophy, smaller C1-C3 MTsat decrease, less M1/S1 R2* increase, and less swelling related to better motor or SCIM outcomes over 5 years.
observations.normalized_value: tissue bridges and remote degeneration metrics predict recovery
observations.statistics_reported: TB increase versus motor recovery n = 16, rho = 0.74, p < 0.001; swelling versus motor recovery n = 22, rho = -0.45, p = 0.04
observations.notes: Bridges local lesion geometry to functional outcome.

observations.evidence_type: Human post-mortem pathology / ventral-root axon counts
observations.observation_value: In chronic human traumatic SCI cases, large-diameter myelinated ventral-root axons were significantly reduced one level above, at, and one to three levels below the epicenter, indicating motoneuron death and muscle denervation risk.
observations.normalized_value: chronic human SCI causes segment-spanning motoneuron loss
observations.notes: Cases had chronic SCI >=6 months post-injury.

observations.observation_value: Large-diameter axons with thin myelin were evident in ventral roots at and below the lesion epicenter; g-ratio increased significantly for large fibers at the epicenter relative to above-lesion roots.
observations.normalized_value: incomplete remyelination in chronic human ventral roots
observations.statistics_reported: location p = 0.027; fiber diameter p < 0.001; interaction p < 0.001; large-fiber epicenter p <= 0.003
observations.notes: Adds myelin pathology to structural lesion profile.

observations.observation_value: In 19 children with chronic SCI and 36 typically developing controls, both SCIWOMR and MRI-positive groups showed abnormal FA and RD values in adjacent MRI-normal-appearing cephalad and caudal spinal cord segments.
observations.normalized_value: pediatric chronic SCI has DTI abnormalities beyond conventional MRI lesion
observations.statistics_reported: Abstract/source-page level; exact values not extracted
observations.notes: Includes traumatic and nontraumatic chronic SCI, >=6 months post-injury.

observations.evidence_type: Pediatric spinal cord DTI / clinical severity
observations.observation_value: In the SCIWOMR subgroup, FA values showed perilesional abnormalities in middle and proximal cephalad and caudal cord segments when AIS A/B children were compared with AIS C/D children.
observations.normalized_value: DTI perilesional abnormality relates to AIS severity
observations.statistics_reported: Abstract/source-page level; exact values not extracted
observations.notes: Useful translational imaging bridge, but full text needed for numeric DTI values.

observations.observation_value: Resected complete chronic SCI scar tissue was CSPG/vimentin-positive and NF/nestin/Tuj-1/S100-negative, providing human tissue evidence for a scar-rich, neural-marker-poor chronic lesion component.
observations.normalized_value: human chronic scar component is inhibitory-matrix/stromal-marker rich
observations.statistics_reported: qualitative IHC
observations.notes: Duplicates tissue fact from T005 in a human-pathology context.

observations.observation_value: PLX5622 pre-treatment reduced spinal microglia, infiltrating myeloid cells, monocytes, neutrophils, immune-cell ROS production, CD11b expression, and TNFalpha upregulation at 2 DPI after thoracic contusion.
observations.normalized_value: CSF1R inhibition dampens acute lesion-site myeloid inflammation
observations.statistics_reported: figure-level statistics reported; n values per group reported
observations.notes: Pre-injury depletion design is a boundary condition.

observations.observation_value: PLX5622 started after SCI and continued to 7 DPI significantly reduced microglia and infiltrating myeloid counts in the spinal impact site and reduced microglia/myeloid populations in the brain.
observations.normalized_value: post-injury CSF1R inhibition reduces acute spinal and brain myeloid populations
observations.statistics_reported: n = 4-5 per group; p values reported in figure
observations.notes: Supports myeloid contribution to leukocyte infiltration after injury.

observations.observation_value: Long-term post-injury PLX5622 did not significantly improve gross BMS locomotion, but improved CatWalk step-sequence regularity and stride length and improved cognitive/depressive-like behavior readouts at 5-6 WPI.
observations.normalized_value: microglial depletion improves selected fine motor and brain-linked behavioral outcomes
observations.statistics_reported: regularity p = 0.0056; stride length p = 0.0162; NOR/Y-maze/TS/FS p values reported
observations.notes: Outcome benefit is not a simple gross locomotion/lesion-volume effect.

observations.observation_value: At 6 WPI after severe contusion, PLX5622 reduced CX3CR1-GFP-positive cells by more than 90% but did not significantly change GFAP-positive area, lesion volume, spared white matter, or BMS; it reduced neuronal loss in thalamus and dentate gyrus.
observations.normalized_value: chronic microglial depletion changes remote neurodegeneration more than lesion volume
observations.statistics_reported: GFP reduction and neuronal-density statistics reported
observations.notes: Important boundary between lesion architecture preservation and remote brain pathology.

observations.observation_value: At 6 WPI, spinal cord NanoString showed 384 injury genes; 218 were PLX-modified and nearly all were attenuated, with strongest effects in inflammatory signaling, innate immune response, microglia function, NF-kappaB, cytokine signaling, adaptive immunity, apoptosis, astrocyte function, growth-factor signaling, and matrix remodeling.
observations.normalized_value: chronic lesion transcriptome has microglia-linked inflammatory/apoptotic/matrix components
observations.statistics_reported: adjusted p < 0.05; 218/384 injury genes PLX-modified
observations.notes: qPCR validated microglial receptors, apoptosis/autophagy markers, and reduced Gfap after PLX.

observations.observation_value: SCI altered cortical neuronal/synaptic, apoptosis, and microglia-function genes at 6 WPI; PLX reduced injury-induced Il1a and Lrrc25 and was associated with improved neuronal survival and neurobehavioral outcomes.
observations.normalized_value: chronic SCI-linked brain inflammation is microglia-modifiable
observations.statistics_reported: cortex DE genes and qPCR statistics reported
observations.notes: Extends immune-glial lesion response to remote brain pathology.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that injury-activated microglia/macrophages acquire temporal IAM signatures and that myeloid Plexin-B2 deletion disrupts wound corralling, matrix compaction, astrocytic border containment, inflammatory restriction, axon regeneration, and recovery.
observations.normalized_value: myeloid cells can promote lesion containment and compaction
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Also supports T003 astrocytic border profile.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that single-cell RNA-seq resolved injured-spinal-cord scar-like cell heterogeneity and inferred cell-cell interactions shaping the wound-healing/scar environment.
observations.normalized_value: lesion scar is multicellular immune-glial/stromal interaction ecosystem
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Shared with T001 but placed here for interaction ecology.

observations.observation_value: Source-page extraction reports that CSF1R inhibition after compression SCI reduced microglia/macrophage proliferation and inflammatory response, promoted tissue preservation, and improved motor recovery.
observations.normalized_value: CSF1R-pathway modulation can preserve tissue and improve recovery
observations.statistics_reported: Source-page level in Module 1A
observations.notes: Directionally concordant with Li but needs full-text extraction for treatment window and lesion metrics.

observations.observation_value: In 12 humans with chronic SCI 2-34 years posttrauma, focal cystic atrophy at the impact site replaced a mean longitudinal area of 2.01 +/- 0.60 cm2, less than one spinal cord segment, corresponding to 89.3 +/- 17.4% loss of one-segment longitudinal area.
observations.normalized_value: chronic human atrophy is focal and cystic at impact site
observations.statistics_reported: quantitative stereology; group summary reported
observations.notes: PDF supplied by user; extracted to data/raw/evidence/M1A-P018.

observations.observation_value: Adjacent spinal cord segments retained substantial area: immediately rostral segments showed 19.4 +/- 7.5% atrophy and caudal segments 16.5 +/- 4.1% atrophy versus normal, leaving about 80.6% rostral and 83.5% caudal area; extensive remote atrophy occurred in 2/12 cases and was attributed to late syrinx formation.
observations.normalized_value: adjacent chronic cord mass largely preserved except syrinx cases
observations.statistics_reported: rostral p < 0.05; caudal p < 0.005; 2/12 syrinx cases
observations.notes: Supports bridging short cystic lesion zones plus long remaining parenchyma concept.

observations.observation_value: In three uninjured human cervical spinal cords, the sulcal arterial system supplied all anterior gray matter, anterior half of posterior gray matter, approximately inner half of anterior/lateral white columns, and anterior half of posterior white columns, with watershed overlap with pial/posterior spinal artery supply.
observations.normalized_value: human central cord vascular territory mapped to sulcal arterial system
observations.statistics_reported: n = 3 uninjured cords; sulcal artery counts reported
observations.notes: Provides vascular anatomy for interpreting central gray/white matter injury patterns.

observations.observation_value: Acute human SCI specimens showed severe hemorrhage predominantly in gray matter with surrounding white matter lesions, including decreased staining, disrupted myelin, axonal/periaxonal swelling, and lesions extending far from the injury site; large anterior/posterior/sulcal arteries remained patent, while occluded intramedullary veins were found in degenerated posterior white columns.
observations.normalized_value: acute vascular injury produces central hemorrhagic necrosis and remote white matter degeneration without large-artery occlusion
observations.statistics_reported: 9 traumatized cords overall; acute cases 20 minutes-18 days
observations.notes: Authors implicate sulcal arterial damage and small-vessel/venous obstruction rather than large artery occlusion.

observations.observation_value: In later human SCI specimens 3-9 months postinjury, injured segments showed major tissue loss with large cavitations, while rostral and caudal remote sites showed well-demarcated necrotic infarct-like areas mainly in posterior white columns.
observations.normalized_value: chronic human vascular pathology includes cavitation and remote posterior-column infarcts
observations.statistics_reported: chronic/subacute cases up to 9 months
observations.notes: Adds vascular mechanism context to human chronic lesion architecture.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic rat hemisection SCI scar has decreased Young's modulus, and scar softening is associated with cavitation, CNS cell loss/dysregulation, dense nuclei packing, and GAG/HA dysregulation.
author_claims.notes: Claim is directly aligned with Fig 1-Fig 4 observations.

author_claims.claim_text: Differences between chronic scar mechanics studies likely depend on injury model, time point, sample preparation, and AFM methodology.
author_claims.notes: Boundary-condition claim explicitly discusses Cooper 2020 versus Baumann 2020.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic severe mouse contusion SCI produces mechanical stiffening of spinal lesion tissue compared with uninjured tissue.
author_claims.notes: Directly supported by AFM lesion measurements.

author_claims.claim_text: Fibrotic scarring is a key driver of chronic mechanical stiffening after SCI, because immune-modifying nanoparticle treatment reduces fibrotic scarring and rescues lesion stiffness.
author_claims.notes: Supported by IMP intervention but still mechanistic because IMP has immune and scar effects.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic lesion stiffness is biologically relevant because mouse and human astrocytes respond to substrate stiffness values in the injured/uninjured spinal cord range.
author_claims.notes: Supported by in vitro substrate observations; extrapolation to in vivo phenotype remains interpretive.

author_claims.claim_text: In contusive SCI with largely intact dura, Col1alpha1-positive perivascular fibroblasts are a major source of the fibrotic scar and are distinct from NG2-positive pericytes.
author_claims.notes: Strongly supported by Col1alpha1-GFP mapping, immunophenotyping, LSFM, and NG2 lineage tracing.

author_claims.claim_text: Fibrotic scar origin and geometry differ with injury type because penetrating injuries that disrupt dura can add meningeal continuity not seen in closed-dura contusion.
author_claims.notes: Supported by dorsal hemisection versus contusion comparison.

author_claims.claim_text: Type A pericyte-derived stromal cells are required for fibrotic scar tissue generation and broad injury-induced ECM/fibrosis gene expression after SCI.
author_claims.notes: Supported by Glast-Rasless attenuation, histology, RNA-seq, qPCR/protein validation.

author_claims.claim_text: Moderate attenuation of pericyte-derived fibrotic scarring promotes CST and raphespinal axon regeneration, circuit integration, and sensorimotor recovery after SCI.
author_claims.notes: Supported across tracing, optogenetics, electrophysiology, behavior, and correlation analyses.

author_claims.claim_text: Complete blockade of fibrotic scar formation is harmful because the fibrotic response is needed for tissue closure; the therapeutic window is partial attenuation rather than ablation.
author_claims.notes: Based on Tam-def animals with tissue defects and poor regeneration/recovery.

author_claims.claim_text: Fibrotic scars after transection and crush SCI primarily derive from perivascular and meningeal fibroblasts rather than pericytes/vSMCs in the authors' lineage-tracing system.
author_claims.notes: Directly boundary-checks the pericyte-origin model using Myh11, NG2, Col1a2, and Crabp2 lineage tools.

author_claims.claim_text: Fibrotic scar tissue is heterogeneous: central CE-F and peripheral/perivascular LA-F occupy distinct scar regions and have different ECM, lipid, and angiogenesis-associated programs.
author_claims.notes: Supported in mouse and monkey datasets plus functional assays.

author_claims.claim_text: The glial scar persists into the chronic phase through scar-forming astrocytes that maintain a robust N-cadherin-associated phenotype while changing some gene-expression features over time.
author_claims.notes: Supported by contusion time course, LMD-qPCR, and chronic 12 WPI analysis.

author_claims.claim_text: Established scar-forming astrocytes are not readily reverted by removing collagen signaling or blocking beta1-integrin/N-cadherin in vitro, but beta1-integrin blockade can reduce recruitment of new scar-forming astrocytes in vivo.
author_claims.notes: Important distinction between state maintenance and recruitment.

author_claims.claim_text: Reducing beta1-integrin-dependent recruitment of chronic scar-forming astrocytes can shrink the glial scar and promote axonal regeneration markers after SCI.
author_claims.notes: GAP43-positive area is a regeneration-associated marker, not a full functional recovery endpoint.

author_claims.claim_text: Injury-activated microglia/macrophages promote corralling, wound compaction, astrocytic border containment, and recovery through Plexin-B2.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; full-text extraction still needed.

author_claims.claim_text: Astrocytic Ryk signaling coordinates astrocyte morphological transformation, scarring, wound healing, and lesion-core containment after SCI.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; full-text extraction still needed.

author_claims.claim_text: Border-forming wound repair astrocytes are an active, transcriptionally reprogrammed repair state derived mainly from local astrocytes with a minority OPC-derived contribution.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; full-text extraction still needed.

author_claims.claim_text: SCI scar architecture is spatially heterogeneous and multicellular, with fibroblast-rich central regions, microglial/astrocytic boundary layers, endothelial/oligodendrocyte-associated compartments, and time-dependent maturation.
author_claims.notes: Supported by spatial transcriptomic atlas, marker maps, and cell-fraction analyses.

author_claims.claim_text: Scar formation follows a staged process in the lateral hemisection model: macrophage infiltration, proliferation/differentiation of scar-resident cells, scar emergence, and stationary scar.
author_claims.notes: Author synthesis from ST time course; should be model-specific.

author_claims.claim_text: FnEDA is required for long-term stability of the assembled fibronectin matrix and for the chronic fibrotic scar after contusive SCI, without being required for acute fibrotic response or GFAP glial scarring.
author_claims.notes: Strongly supported by FnEDA-null matrix fractionation and chronic lesion histology.

author_claims.claim_text: Reducing chronic FnEDA-dependent fibrotic scarring improves axonal density and locomotor recovery after contusive SCI.
author_claims.notes: Supported by genotype comparison; causal inference should stay tied to FnEDA-null model.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic contusion repair tissue can be described as fibrotic, cellular, and axonal zones, and combined scar ablation/transplantation can alter zone integration and cavity morphology.
author_claims.notes: Abstract-level only; useful as historical/zone-architecture support.

author_claims.claim_text: The injured spinal cord scar environment is a heterogeneous multicellular ecosystem whose cell-cell interactions shape wound healing and inhibitory scar features.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; full text still needed.

author_claims.claim_text: Spared lesion-remote white matter is an active repair compartment where region-specific astrocyte states regulate debris-clearing microglia and neurological recovery after SCI.
author_claims.notes: Strongly supported by transcriptomics, spatial mapping, conditional deletion, debris assays, and behavior.

author_claims.claim_text: Astrocyte-derived CCN1 coordinates white matter repair by restricting excessive microglial activation and supporting lipid/debris processing in Wallerian-degenerating tracts.
author_claims.notes: Supported by Ccn1-cKO, CCN1 protein/secreted factor, debris, and lipid assays.

author_claims.claim_text: White matter sparing can be detected by high-resolution MRI and aligns with histology and locomotor recovery after chronic rat SCI.
author_claims.notes: Source-page/abstract-level details; n = 5.

author_claims.claim_text: In vivo MRI can track white matter tract pathology after severe thoracic SCI and relate spared white matter to hindlimb recovery.
author_claims.notes: Needs full-text metric extraction.

author_claims.claim_text: Residual white matter undergoes chronic cellular-composition changes after contusion SCI, making spared funiculi a lesion-adjacent compartment rather than normal tissue.
author_claims.notes: Abstract-level only.

author_claims.claim_text: Glial scar resection alone is not uniformly beneficial after SCI; it can be neutral after dorsal hemisection and harmful after contusion, implying that the scar can have protective/stabilizing functions.
author_claims.notes: Strong boundary-condition claim for scaffold/resection strategies.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic scar resection can be used as a preparation step for NeuroRegen collagen scaffold plus hUC-MSC implantation, with outcomes assessed by locomotion, electrophysiology, and histological repair.
author_claims.notes: Abstract-level only until full text is parsed.

author_claims.claim_text: In complete chronic SCI patients, electrophysiology-guided scar resection followed by BMMC-loaded NeuroRegen scaffold implantation is feasible and did not produce obvious procedure-related adverse effects in a small one-year cohort.
author_claims.notes: Keep scoped to five ASIA A patients and feasibility/safety, not proven efficacy.

author_claims.claim_text: Human chronic resection specimens can be scar-rich and neural-marker-poor, supporting careful scar targeting in complete chronic cases but highlighting a challenge for incomplete injuries where scar and spared neural tissue may mix.
author_claims.notes: Directly tied to CSPG/vimentin-positive and neural-marker-negative specimen plus authors' incomplete-injury caution.

author_claims.claim_text: Scaffold-supported filling of chronic post-resection cavitary lesions, including dECM hydrogel with human iPSC-derived NS/PCs, is a recurring histological repair strategy in chronic complete SCI models.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; exact histological endpoints need full-text extraction.

author_claims.claim_text: Human spinal lesion geometry undergoes rapid early demarcation, then remains relatively stable from 1 month to at least 5 years, while remote motor-system degeneration continues and relates to recovery.
author_claims.notes: Strong longitudinal MRI evidence.

author_claims.claim_text: Midsagittal tissue bridges and remote MRI metrics are clinically meaningful human lesion-architecture variables because they associate with motor and functional recovery over 5 years.
author_claims.notes: Author interpretation supported by correlations and GLMs.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic human SCI produces segment-spanning motoneuron loss and muscle denervation risk, measurable as reduced large-diameter myelinated axons in ventral roots around the lesion.
author_claims.notes: Direct post-mortem pathology evidence.

author_claims.claim_text: Thin myelin on large ventral-root axons at or below the epicenter indicates incomplete remyelination as a chronic human SCI pathology feature.
author_claims.notes: Directly supported by g-ratio analysis.

author_claims.claim_text: Pediatric chronic SCI can show DTI abnormalities in adjacent MRI-normal-appearing cord, and FA abnormalities can relate to AIS severity even when conventional MRI is normal.
author_claims.notes: Source-page/abstract-level until full text values are parsed.

author_claims.claim_text: Human chronic complete SCI scar tissue can be CSPG/vimentin-positive and neural-marker-poor, providing tissue-level support for a persistent scar-rich component in chronic lesions.
author_claims.notes: Same tissue observation also supports T005 scar-resection boundary.

author_claims.claim_text: CSF1R inhibition with PLX5622 reduces acute spinal cord microglia/myeloid infiltration, ROS, and inflammatory cytokine expression after contusion SCI.
author_claims.notes: Supported by pre- and post-injury treatment experiments.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic microglia-linked neuroinflammation after SCI modifies spinal cord and brain transcriptomes, astrocyte/matrix/apoptosis programs, remote neuronal survival, and selected neurological behaviors.
author_claims.notes: Captures spinal and remote brain components without claiming reduced lesion volume.

author_claims.claim_text: Beneficial immune modulation is timing- and function-dependent because early microglia may support neuroprotection/repair, while sustained neurotoxic activation contributes to chronic pathology.
author_claims.notes: Explicit author discussion; important boundary for T008 consensus.

author_claims.claim_text: Injury-activated microglia/macrophages can be required for lesion corralling, wound compaction, astrocytic containment, inflammatory restriction, axon regeneration, and recovery through Plexin-B2.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; full-text extraction needed before high-confidence materialization.

author_claims.claim_text: The injured spinal cord scar environment is a multicellular interaction ecosystem whose immune, glial, and stromal cell states communicate during wound healing and scar formation.
author_claims.notes: Shared conceptual bridge with T001.

author_claims.claim_text: CSF1R-pathway inhibition can reduce microglia/macrophage proliferation and inflammation while promoting tissue preservation and motor recovery after SCI.
author_claims.notes: Source-page level; treatment timing/details needed.

author_claims.claim_text: Chronic human traumatic SCI usually preserves substantial spinal cord mass adjacent to a relatively short focal cystic atrophy zone, with extensive remote atrophy mainly occurring when late syrinx formation develops.
author_claims.notes: Full text supplied by user.

author_claims.claim_text: Neural repair strategies in chronic human SCI should account for bridging short cystic atrophy zones while supporting axonal growth through longer remaining cord parenchyma.
author_claims.notes: Translational interpretation; not a direct therapeutic experiment.

author_claims.claim_text: Human SCI vascular pathology is centered on sulcal-artery/small-vessel territory damage, gray-matter hemorrhagic necrosis, surrounding white-matter degeneration, and later central myelomalacia/cavitation rather than complete occlusion of major spinal arteries.
author_claims.notes: Full text supplied by user.

author_claims.claim_text: Remote rostral and caudal white-matter necrosis/infarction, especially in posterior columns, is part of human SCI vascular pathology and should be represented separately from local impact-site cavitation.
author_claims.notes: Supports separate local and remote pathology fields.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Chronic SCI lesion architecture is a multicompartment structure, not a single scar. The core evidence supports separately queryable fibrotic or ECM-rich matrix, astrocytic border, immune/myeloid containment zone, spared or reactive neural tissue, altered white matter, and cystic or repaired-tissue cavities when present.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Supported by spatial transcriptomics and scar-matrix full-text evidence, with lower-confidence abstract/source-page support for chronic zone architecture and single-cell lesion ecology; observations M1B-O037-M1B-O046; claims M1B-C019-M1B-C024.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: Compartment definitions vary by model, time point, assay resolution, and whether lesions are contusive, hemisection, cavitary, or scaffold-repaired; do not collapse all scar biology into one field.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Chronic SCI lesion architecture is a multicompartment structure, not a single scar. The core evidence supports separately queryable fibrotic or ECM-rich matrix, astrocytic border, immune/myeloid containment zone, spared or reactive neural tissue, altered white matter, and cystic or repaired-tissue cavities when present.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: SCI fibrotic scarring arises from perivascular and meningeal-associated stromal lineages, but lineage assignment depends on marker system, Cre driver, injury geometry, dural disruption, and endpoint. The consensus is modulation, not ablation: partial attenuation can support repair in some models, whereas loss of closure-producing stroma can be harmful.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Supported by three full-text lineage/function studies; observations M1B-O012-M1B-O026; claims M1B-C006-M1B-C012.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: M1B-X002 resolves apparent pericyte-versus-fibroblast conflicts as marker/system dependent; store marker, lineage driver, injury model, and dural status with each claim.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: SCI fibrotic scarring arises from perivascular and meningeal-associated stromal lineages, but lineage assignment depends on marker system, Cre driver, injury geometry, dural disruption, and endpoint. The consensus is modulation, not ablation: partial attenuation can support repair in some models, whereas loss of closure-producing stroma can be harmful.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Astrocytic scar borders are active wound-repair and containment structures with separable programs for formation, maintenance, recruitment, and morphology. Established scar-forming astrocytes may resist direct reversal, while border recruitment and organization remain intervention-sensitive in vivo.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Strongest support comes from Tamaru 2023 full text, with source-page support from immune-corralling, Ryk, and wound-repair astrocyte studies; observations M1B-O027-M1B-O036; claims M1B-C013-M1B-C018.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: M1B-X003 distinguishes established astrocyte-state reversal from border recruitment/organization. Source-page studies should remain medium confidence until full-text upgrades.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Astrocytic scar borders are active wound-repair and containment structures with separable programs for formation, maintenance, recruitment, and morphology. Established scar-forming astrocytes may resist direct reversal, while border recruitment and organization remain intervention-sensitive in vivo.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Chronic SCI changes lesion mechanics, but stiffness direction is model- and method-dependent. No universal chronic-scar stiffness claim should be encoded without species, injury model, time point, tissue preparation, AFM method, and lesion subregion.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Supported by two full-text mechanics studies with directly conflicting directionality; observations M1B-O001-M1B-O011; claims M1B-C001-M1B-C005.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: M1B-X001 is the central synthesis: rat hemisection scar softening and mouse contusion lesion stiffening are both admissible under different model/method conditions.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Chronic SCI changes lesion mechanics, but stiffness direction is model- and method-dependent. No universal chronic-scar stiffness claim should be encoded without species, injury model, time point, tissue preparation, AFM method, and lesion subregion.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Chronic scar resection divides into distinct intervention classes: tissue removal alone and resection followed by cavity or gap reconstruction. Resection alone is not consensus-reparative and may be harmful in contusion models, whereas scaffold or cell filling is a recurring but model- and endpoint-dependent reconstruction strategy.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Supported by two full-text resection/human scaffold studies plus abstract/source-page scaffold support; observations M1B-O056-M1B-O062; claims M1B-C030-M1B-C034.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: M1B-X004 resolves the apparent conflict by distinguishing tissue removal from reconstruction after resection; efficacy claims should not be generalized beyond feasibility/safety or specific animal endpoints.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Chronic scar resection divides into distinct intervention classes: tissue removal alone and resection followed by cavity or gap reconstruction. Resection alone is not consensus-reparative and may be harmful in contusion models, whereas scaffold or cell filling is a recurring but model- and endpoint-dependent reconstruction strategy.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Spared and lesion-remote white matter is an active recovery-linked compartment rather than normal residual tissue. It can carry chronic degeneration, repair-associated glial and immune remodeling, MRI-visible tract changes, debris and lipid-handling programs, and behavioral associations.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Strongest support comes from McCallum 2026 full text, with imaging and abstract support from Scholtes, Song, and Rosenberg; observations M1B-O047-M1B-O055; claims M1B-C025-M1B-C029.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: Boundary conditions include tract, distance from lesion, injury severity, post-mortem versus in vivo imaging, and exact MRI/histology metric. Lower-access white matter papers should not carry high-confidence numeric claims yet.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Spared and lesion-remote white matter is an active recovery-linked compartment rather than normal residual tissue. It can carry chronic degeneration, repair-associated glial and immune remodeling, MRI-visible tract changes, debris and lipid-handling programs, and behavioral associations.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Human chronic SCI lesion architecture is multi-scale and should retain separate fields for local lesion geometry and tissue bridges, focal cystic atrophy, retained adjacent parenchyma, vascular territory and remote infarct-like pathology, scar-marker composition, segmental motoneuron/root pathology, remyelination status, perilesional DTI abnormalities, and remote motor-system degeneration.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Supported by five full-text human studies plus source-page pediatric DTI support; observations M1B-O063-M1B-O070 and M1B-O080-M1B-O084; claims M1B-C035-M1B-C040 and M1B-C047-M1B-C050.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: M1B-X005 and M1B-X007 separate local lesion stability/adjacent mass preservation from remote degeneration, vascular infarct-like lesions, and syrinx-associated atrophy.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Human chronic SCI lesion architecture is multi-scale and should retain separate fields for local lesion geometry and tissue bridges, focal cystic atrophy, retained adjacent parenchyma, vascular territory and remote infarct-like pathology, scar-marker composition, segmental motoneuron/root pathology, remyelination status, perilesional DTI abnormalities, and remote motor-system degeneration.

consensus_drafts.draft_consensus_statement: Immune-glial lesion biology is phase-, cell-, function-, and endpoint-specific. Myeloid and glial responses can support corralling, compaction, containment, debris handling, and repair while also contributing to chronic neuroinflammatory pathology and remote effects.
consensus_drafts.supporting_evidence_scope: Supported by Li 2020 full text plus source-page evidence from Zhou, Milich, and Gerber; observations M1B-O071-M1B-O079; claims M1B-C041-M1B-C046.
consensus_drafts.boundary_contradiction_handling: M1B-X006 resolves the main tension as phase- and function-specific: protective early containment/debris roles can coexist with chronic inflammatory harm. Source-page mechanistic rows remain medium confidence.
consensus_drafts.consensus_statement: Immune-glial lesion biology is phase-, cell-, function-, and endpoint-specific. Myeloid and glial responses can support corralling, compaction, containment, debris handling, and repair while also contributing to chronic neuroinflammatory pathology and remote effects.
